- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# BRIEF V3 — THE CELLCOG PROFILE. Design POLARIS's path to the TOP of the board (0.5578).
2	# READ THE DEBIASING SECTION FIRST. Your previous plan was tunnel-visioned and the operator caught it.
3	
4	## 0. WHAT YOU GOT WRONG LAST TIME (both designers, independently — do not repeat it)
5	1. **YOU BOTH ANCHORED ON "REWRITE THE BANKED REPORT".** Both plans took `outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md`
6	   as the substrate and designed a report->report retrofit. That framing CAPPED both plans at ~0.48-0.51 and both of
7	   you then reported "we cannot reach 0.54" as if it were a fact about the world. **It was a fact about your framing.**
8	2. **YOU BOTH ACCEPTED A TRADE-OFF YOU NEVER MEASURED: "sources vs thinking" / "breadth vs depth".** Fable capped
9	   length and proposed DELETING words to raise density. Sol proposed shrinking to 5-7k words. **The #1 system does the
10	   opposite of both: it has 98 sources (we have 97), writes 13,580 body words, AND is mostly analysis.** There is no
11	   trade-off. The operator spotted this; neither of you did.
12	3. **YOU BOTH MISSED THE SINGLE CHEAPEST LEVER ON THE BOARD** (see §2). It was sitting in the artifacts you both read.
13	4. Fable additionally mis-measured cellcog's citation rate (called it "92% uncited" — it is not uncited, it is
14	   cited IN PROSE) and built a whole argument on it. **Verify your own measurements against the raw text.**
15	
16	**METHOD RULE FOR THIS ROUND:** every claim you make must be re-derived from the artifacts at
17	`/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/` or the grader source. Do not inherit a premise from this brief
18	or from your last plan without checking it. If you find THIS brief is wrong, say so — that is the most valuable thing
19	you can do.
20	
21	## 1. THE TARGET: THE CELLCOG PROFILE (the #1 system, 0.5578)
22	| | cellcog-max (#1) | bodhi (task-72 winner) | **POLARIS** |
23	|---|---|---|---|
24	| overall | **0.5578** | 0.5441 | **0.4382** |
25	| distinct sources | **98** | 33 | **97** |
26	| body words | **13,580** | 4,361 | 7,742 |
27	| H3 subsections | 31 | 40 | **0** |
28	| median paragraph | 75w | 59w | **677w** |
29	| **authors named IN PROSE** | **133 (9.8/1k words)** | ~54 | **10 (1.3/1k)** |
30	| **journals named IN PROSE** | **65** | 1 | **1** |
31	| `[n]` citation markers | **0** | 105 | **240** |
32	**cellcog has OUR source count, writes 1.75x our length, and is mostly ANALYSIS. The "breadth vs depth" trade-off is FALSE.**
33	Two winning shapes exist (bodhi: 33 sources / 4.4k words / half analysis; cellcog: 98 sources / 13.6k words / mostly
34	analysis). **CELLCOG'S SHAPE IS THE ONE THAT MATCHES OUR STRENGTH — we already have the retrieval depth. We are simply
35	unable to REASON over what we retrieve.**
36	
37	## 2. THE CHEAPEST LEVER ON THE BOARD — BOTH OF YOU WALKED PAST IT
38	**RACE STRIPS EVERY `[n]` MARKER AND THE ENTIRE REFERENCE LIST BEFORE THE JUDGE READS ANYTHING**
39	(`third_party/deep_research_bench/utils/clean_article.py` — verified: our 345 markers -> 0, our 105-entry bibliography -> deleted).
40	- **We put ALL our sourcing into `[n]` markers (240 of them). The judge sees NONE of it.**
41	- **cellcog writes `Acemoglu and Restrepo (2018), in the *American Economic Review*, show...` — that is JUST WORDS IN A
42	  SENTENCE. The cleaner cannot touch it. It SURVIVES.**
43	- => **The judge reads cellcog as a rigorously sourced scholarly review, and reads POLARIS as a report with NO SOURCES AT ALL.**
44	- This is on a task whose instruction is literally *"Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal
45	  articles."* **We cite 97 journal articles. The judge sees zero.** Our instruction-following: 0.4409. cellcog: 0.5530.
46	- **WE ALREADY HOLD THE DATA**: `outputs/rank10_sections_compose/bibliography.json` has `authors[]` and venue per entry.
47	  Converting `[n]` markers into in-prose `Author (Year, *Journal*)` attribution is a DETERMINISTIC, FABRICATION-PROOF
48	  transform (every injected token is a byte-substring of the sidecar record). **It is nearly free and neither of you proposed it.**
49	
50	## 3. THE REAL BOTTLENECK (this is what actually caps us, not length, not sources)
51	`verify_sentence_provenance` (provenance_generator.py:2098) admits a sentence only if `len(failures)==0`. The entailment
52	judge's NEUTRAL clause (entailment_judge.py:588) kills any sentence introducing *"a fact, entity, **MECHANISM** ... NOT
53	present in the SPAN"*.
54	- A cross-source inference IS, by definition, a mechanism in neither span. **Our gate deletes exactly the sentence type
55	  the 0.32-weight INSIGHT dimension pays for.**
56	- Boundary: a CONJUNCTION of two sources passes ("X found 2.1% [a] while Y found 0.4% [b]"). Add one interpretive clause
57	  ("a gap that may reflect slower adoption") and it is KILLED.
58	- **This is why we stop at 7,742 words: a fact conveyor RUNS OUT OF THINGS TO SAY. Our length is a SYMPTOM, not a choice.**
59	  cellcog writes 13,580 words off the same evidence because it can REASON across it.
60	- **FAITHFULNESS IS THE MOAT AND IS NOT FOR SALE.** No fabricated fact, number, quote, or attribution may EVER ship.
61	  But note what cellcog's synthesis prose actually is — verbatim:
62	    *"The three frameworks are complementary rather than competitive. SBTC remains the clearest model for relative skill
63	    demand... The task-based framework subsumes the phenomena both describe within a more general model... A rigorous
64	    reading of the field therefore treats these as layered lenses to be deployed according to the question at hand."*
65	  **That contains NO new fact, NO number, NO new entity. It RANKS AND RELATES ideas already on the page. It is
66	  ADJUDICATION, and it is FULLY COMPATIBLE with a no-fabrication guarantee.** Design for THAT.
67	
68	## 4. WHAT THE GRADER PAYS FOR (read from its source, not inferred)
69	- ONE call scores our report and the reference SIDE BY SIDE, writing a comparative analysis BEFORE the numbers, with all
70	  ~25 criteria in ONE context => **cross-dimension bleed is real**: our 677-word walls tax the other 86% of the score.
71	- **Weights: INSIGHT 0.32 | COMPREHENSIVENESS 0.29 | INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING 0.25 | READABILITY 0.14.** Insight+compreh = 61-64%.
72	- Scale 0-10 per criterion. Bands: 4-6 Average, **6-8 Good**, **8-10 Excellent**. We read ~6.6; the reference ~9.0.
73	  **To beat parity a criterion must cross from Good into Excellent. No credit for being a better 7.**
74	- **Overall = ours/(ours+reference)** — COMPRESSIVE: +1.0 raw on EVERY criterion = only +0.036 overall.
75	- **LENGTH IS NEVER MENTIONED IN THE SCORING PROMPT.** Padding is penalised at ~0.7% of overall; substance-bearing
76	  expansion pays into 61-64%. **=> "longer with more FACTS" is dead (we proved it: ranks 7-12, +5,800 words, score flat).
77	  "longer with more ANALYSIS" is how the #1 system wins.** Do not confuse these two again.
78	- **HEDGING IS NOT REWARDED. ADJUDICATION IS**: "Critical Evaluation of Evidence and Synthesis of Competing Theories",
79	  "explains causal relationships behind phenomena", "rather than merely cataloging effects", "identifies key issues...
80	  provides insightful solutions", "forward-looking thinking".
81	- Comprehensiveness = breadth AND depth AND **evidence** AND "multiple perspectives and balance" — NOT "more sections".
82	- Executive summaries: NOT rewarded (the reference ships an EMPTY PLACEHOLDER one and still beats everybody).
83	- Bullets/tables: NOT the lever (sourcery ships 106 bullets and beats the reference; cellcog ships 0 bullets, 0 tables).
84	
85	## 5. MEASUREMENT (pinned tonight — never score n=1 again)
86	- Judge noise SD = **0.0074** (byte-identical artifact scored 6x). k=5 paired => SE(diff) 0.0047 => **smallest resolvable
87	  effect = +0.0094 at 2 sigma.**
88	- Generation noise (re-composing the same config) ~0.016 — applies ONLY to arms needing a new compose.
89	- Our own real history: baseline 0.4062 -> rank10 0.4382 = **+0.032, z=10** (real). Every lever AFTER that (padding
90	  removal, source firewall) scored FLAT — because all of it was invisible to the judge.
91	
92	## 6. YOUR TASK — DESIGN THE PATH TO THE CELLCOG PROFILE
93	Target: **0.5578** (or at minimum bodhi's 0.5441). We are at 0.4382.
94	**Design for the cellcog shape: ~98 sources (we HAVE them) + ~12-16k words + MOSTLY ANALYSIS + in-prose author/journal
95	attribution + 30-40 claim-first H3 + ~75-word paragraphs.**
96	
97	You are NOT restricted to rewriting the banked artifact. **If reaching the cellcog profile requires changing the
98	COMPOSER (how prose is generated) or the RETRIEVAL (what evidence is banked), design that.** Say what must change and
99	where (file:line). A ~65-minute compose is an acceptable cost if the mechanism justifies it.
100	
101	For every lever: the VERBATIM rubric criterion it moves, expected points, how it FAILS, and the CHEAP test that proves
102	the mechanism fired before a full compose. Respect the faithfulness moat absolutely — but note (§3) that cellcog's
103	winning synthesis prose adds NO new facts, so the moat and the target are NOT in conflict.
104	
105	Be honest about the ceiling. And where you are UNCERTAIN, SEARCH (papers, GitHub, and the artifacts on disk).
106	**Do not hand us another proxy metric — we lost an entire night to those.**
107

# The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market: A Peer-Reviewed Literature Review

## Abstract

**Objective.** This review synthesizes peer-reviewed evidence on the restructuring impact of artificial intelligence on labor markets, situating AI as the signature technology of the Fourth Industrial Revolution and asking what the literature supports about its effects on employment, wages, tasks, the labor share, and labor-process autonomy.

**Methods.** The review draws on approximately 96 English-language peer-reviewed journal articles from economics, management and operations, sociology and technology studies, and applied health and education venues, spanning the early 1990s through April 2026. Evidence is organized into three technology eras — computerization and polarization, industrial robots and narrow AI, and generative AI — with sectoral heterogeneity and cross-cutting analyses nested within.

cd /tmp/ver && cat > measure.py <<'EOF'
import re, sys, statistics, json

def body_of(text):
    # split at References heading
    m = re.search(r'^#{1,6}\s*(\d+\.?\s*)?References\s*$', text, re.M|re.I)
    if not m: return text, ''
    return text[:m.start()], text[m.start():]

def metrics(name, text):
    body, refs = body_of(text)
    words = len(re.findall(r'\b[\w\'-]+\b', body))
    h3 = len(re.findall(r'^### ', body, re.M))
    h2 = len(re.findall(r'^## ', body, re.M))
    h4 = len(re.findall(r'^#### ', body, re.M))
    # paragraphs: non-heading, non-blank, non-list blocks
    paras=[]
    for blk in re.split(r'\n\s*\n', body):
        b=blk.strip()
        if not b or b.startswith('#'): continue
        if re.match(r'^([-*+]|\d+\.)\s', b): continue
        if b.startswith('|'): continue
        w=len(re.findall(r'\b[\w\'-]+\b', b))
        if w>=5: paras.append(w)
    # [n] markers in body
    nmark = len(re.findall(r'\[\d+(?:\s*[,–-]\s*\d+)*\]', body))
    nmark_simple = len(re.findall(r'\[\d+\]', body))
    # in-prose author-year: Name (2020) / Name and Name (2020) / Name et al. (2020)
    ay = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][A-Za-zÀ-ſ\'’-]+(?:(?:,| and| &|,? et al\.?)\s+[A-Z]?[A-Za-zÀ-ſ\'’.-]+)*)\s*\((?:19|20)\d{2}[a-z]?\)', body)
    # also parenthetical (Author, 2020) style
    paren = re.findall(r'\([A-Z][A-Za-zÀ-ſ\'’-]+[^()]{0,60}?,\s*(?:19|20)\d{2}[a-z]?\)', body)

# 清洗文章的提示词，去除引用格式、参考文献等内容
clean_article_prompt_zh = """
<system_role>你是一名专业的文章编辑，擅长整理和清洗文章内容。</system_role>

<user_prompt>
下面给你的内容可能是一篇完整研究文章，也可能只是其中的一个连续片段（chunk）。
请只针对输入的这部分内容进行清洗，不要假设、补全或引用片段之外的上下文。

清洗目标：去除所有引用链接、引用标记（如[1]、[2]、1、2 等或其他复杂引用格式）、参考文献列表、脚注，并确保文章内容连贯流畅。
保留文章的所有其他原本内容、只移除引用。如果文章中使用引用标记中的内容作为语句的一部分，保留这其中的文字内容，移除其他标记。

特殊情况：如果输入的这段内容整段都属于某个文章的参考文献区（reference 列表 / 脚注定义等），不包含任何正文，请返回空字符串。

输入内容：
"{article}"

请返回清洗后的全文，不要添加任何额外说明或评论。
</user_prompt>
"""

clean_article_prompt_en = """
<system_role>You are a professional article editor who is good at cleaning and refining article content.</system_role>

<user_prompt>
The input below may be a complete research article or just a continuous fragment (chunk) of one.
Operate only on the content provided; do not assume, complete, or quote any context outside this fragment.

Cleaning goal: remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2], 1, 2, etc. or other complex citation formats), reference lists, footnotes, and ensure the content reads smoothly.
Keep all other original content; remove only citations. If a citation mark wraps content that is part of a sentence, keep the text inside and drop only the marks.

Special case: if the entire input is a reference / bibliography / footnote-definition section of some article with no body text, return an empty string.

Input:
"{article}"

Return the cleaned text in full, without any additional explanation or comment.
</user_prompt>
"""

import os, sys, json, re

DRB = "/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/third_party/deep_research_bench"
sys.path.insert(0, DRB)

# load .env WITHOUT echoing anything
for line in open("/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/.env"):
    line = line.strip()
    if not line or line.startswith("#") or "=" not in line:
        continue

    head=blk.split('(')[0]
    for m in re.finditer(r'([A-Z][a-zA-ZÀ-ſ\'’-]{2,})\s*,\s*(?:[A-Z]\.\s*)+', head):
        surnames.add(m.group(1))
surnames -= {'The','And','In','A'}

# journals (italic spans)

journals={j for j in journals if j.rstrip('.') not in ('Objective','Methods','Findings','Contributions')}

# strip headings, split sentences

sents=[s.strip() for s in re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+(?=[A-Z“"*])', prose) if len(s.split())>=6]

jour_re=re.compile('|'.join(re.escape(j) for j in journals)) if journals else None
year_re=re.compile(r'\((?:19|20)\d{2}[a-z]?\)')

g_name=g_year=g_jour=0
grounded=[]; analysis=[]
for s in sents:
    hn=bool(name_re.search(s)); hy=bool(year_re.search(s)); hj=bool(jour_re.search(s)) if jour_re else False
    if hn or hy or hj:
        grounded.append(s); g_name+=hn; g_year+=hy; g_jour+=hj
    else:
        analysis.append(s)
t=len(sents)
print(f'\ncellcog body sentences: {t}')
print(f'  GROUNDED (names/year/journal): {len(grounded):4d}  ({len(grounded)/t:.1%})')
print(f'  ANALYSIS (no attribution)    : {len(analysis):4d}  ({len(analysis)/t:.1%})')
print(f'    [of grounded: {g_name} have a surname, {g_year} a year, {g_jour} a journal]')

The core intuition of the brief -- 'the judge reads cellcog as sourced and POLARIS as unsourced' -- is REAL and I reproduced it end to end.
cellcog body words = 13,580 (whitespace-split) -- EXACT. POLARIS body words = 7,742 -- EXACT. Both verified after splitting at '## References'.

cellcog H3 = 31 -- EXACT. POLARIS H3 = 0 -- EXACT. POLARIS median paragraph = 677w -- EXACT. POLARIS [n] markers in body = 240 -- EXACT. POLARIS distinct cited sources = 97 (of 105 bib entries; 8 uncited: 15,72,74,76,77,81,89,90) -- EXACT.
cellcog [n] markers = 0 -- EXACT. cellcog journal names = 64 distinct / 131 in-prose mentions (brief said 65) -- confirmed.

'cellcog is mostly ANALYSIS' -- CONFIRMED. Grounded = sentence containing any surname harvested from cellcog's own 104-entry bibliography (269 surnames), OR a (YYYY), OR a journal name -- i.e. author-year in prose COUNTS as grounded, done properly this time. Result: 474 body sentences, 188 grounded (39.7%), 286 pure analysis (60.3%).

Brief SS3 code refs verified exactly: verify_sentence_provenance at src/polaris_graph/generator/provenance_generator.py:2098, and the NEUTRAL clause at src/polaris_graph/llm/entailment_judge.py:588 does contain the word 'mechanism' ('the SENTENCE introduces a fact, entity, mechanism, or specificity NOT present in the SPAN').

Brief SS4 weights verified exactly from third_party/deep_research_bench/data/criteria_data/criteria.jsonl id=72: insight 0.32, comprehensiveness 0.29, instruction_following 0.25, readability 0.14; 25 criteria total (6+5+7+7).

The SS3 'verbatim' cellcog synthesis quote ('The three frameworks are complementary rather than competitive...' / 'A rigorous reading of the field therefore treats these as layered lenses...') is genuinely verbatim -- all four fragments found in the article body.
refuted: ** THE LOAD-BEARING CLAIM IS WRONG IN THE ONE WAY THAT KILLS THE PLAN. ** Brief SS2: 'Converting [n] markers into in-prose Author (Year, *Journal*) attribution is a DETERMINISTIC, FABRICATION-PROOF transform... It is nearly free.' I ran a controlled 4-way probe through the real cleaner (/tmp/ver/probe.py -- same 4 facts, 4 attribution formats). Variant B, the parenthetical form '...tasks (Acemoglu & Restrepo, 2019, Journal of Economic Perspectives).' -> cleaner output retained ZERO author names, ZERO journal names, ZERO years. Byte-identical outcome to the [n] baseline. The cleaner strips it as an 'other complex citation format' (the prompt says exactly that). A deterministic marker->parenthetical substitution -- which is the ONLY transform that IS deterministic -- BUYS EXACTLY NOTHING. Shipping it would be another lost night on a lever that never fires.

** ONLY GRAMMATICAL INTEGRATION SURVIVES, AND THAT IS NOT A DETERMINISTIC TRANSFORM. ** Probe variant C -- 'Acemoglu and Restrepo (2019), writing in the *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, show that automation generates a displacement effect...' -- survived with 6 author names + 4 journal names intact. The attribution survives ONLY when the author is the grammatical SUBJECT/possessive of the clause, so deleting it would break the sentence. It is NOT protected by 'being just words in a sentence' (the brief's stated reason). Proof: cellcog's own Table 1 'Anchor Studies' column, whose cells read 'Acemoglu & Restrepo (2020); Dauth et al. (2021); ...', was EMPTIED to blank by the cleaner -- same words, same article, non-integrated position, deleted. Consequence: the working transform is a generative SENTENCE REWRITE, not a string injection. It is neither deterministic, nor free, nor fabrication-proof by construction -- it needs its own faithfulness gate.

** EVERY YEAR IS DELETED. ** cellcog raw -> cleaned: 281 '(YYYY)' parentheses -> 0 (177 of them in the body -> 0). Not one year reaches the judge, in any of the 4 probe variants either. The brief's target format 'Author (Year, *Journal*)' spends effort on a component with a 100% deletion rate. What actually reaches the judge is 'Acemoglu and Restrepo's commuting-zone study...' -- name + venue, NO year. The brief's '133 in-prose author-YEAR attributions' mis-describes what the judge sees: it sees ~126+ author NAMES and 48+ journal names, and zero years.

** 'WE ALREADY HOLD THE DATA' IS FALSE -- THE SIDECAR IS EMPTY. ** Brief SS2 asserts outputs/rank10_sections_compose/bibliography.json 'has authors[] and venue per entry'. It has the KEYS on all 105 entries but they are empty strings. Actual: authors non-empty on 4/105 (nums 1, 17, 24, 27 -- Acemoglu/Restrepo, Autor, Frey/Osborne, Eloundou). Of the 97 CITED entries: 4/97 have authors, 31/97 a year, 29/97 a venue, and 4/97 have all three. The 'nearly free' transform has 4% coverage -- it would yield ~4 attributions, not 133. Worse, the venues that DO exist are partly junk: 'Review of AI', 'Review of Skill Transformation in the Industry', 'Review of Underlying Concepts and Evidence', 'Review of Economic Policy' are not real journals (title-parsing artifacts), and entry 21 pairs source_title 'Scientific Utopia: II...' with statement 'Digital Transformation... - arXiv' and year 2012 for a 2025 arXiv paper. Injecting these into prose FABRICATES journal names and years -- a direct breach of the faithfulness moat the brief claims the transform respects.

** 'WE CITE 97 JOURNAL ARTICLES. THE JUDGE SEES ZERO.' -- THE FIRST HALF IS FALSE, AND IT INVERTS THE LEVER. ** Of the 97 cited sources, 66 are demonstrably NOT peer-reviewed journal articles: personal academic homepages (shakkednoy.com, emmawiles.com, andershumlum.com, danielle.li, inigomedina.co, lukasbfreund.github.io), a Substack (gerarddevalence.substack.com), bank/vendor marketing (jpmorgan.com, goldmansachs.com, bnpparibas, toptal.com, amplifai.com, apporya.com), news (news.harvard.edu, hrexecutive.com), and working-paper/preprint repos (nber.org, arxiv.org, ssrn, docs.iza.org, researchgate). The [n] markers are currently CONCEALING an instruction-following violation, not concealing a strength. Task-72's criterion 'Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles' carries w=0.15 inside instruction_following (w=0.25). Surfacing our venues in prose without first fixing retrieval would EXPOSE the violation and could LOWER instruction-following -- the exact opposite of the brief's predicted gain.

cellcog's median paragraph is ~100 words, NOT 75. Measured under four conventions: block-split-minus-headings 99.5w, excluding lists/tables 99.0w, blocks >=5w 105w, per-line 92w; excluding the abstract, 101w; median 4 sentences/paragraph. Distribution: p25=70, p50=100, p75=124, max 273. 75w is roughly cellcog's 25th-30th percentile. A design targeting '~75-word paragraphs' is targeting something ~30% tighter than the actual #1 system.
cellcog's in-prose attribution count is ~170, not 133 (159 narrative 'Author (Year)' + 11 parenthetical '(Author, Year)'; 177 total '(YYYY)' parens in body). The brief undercounts by ~28%.

'cellcog ships 0 bullets, 0 tables' is FALSE. It ships 0 bullets but DOES ship a table: 'Table 1. Sectoral Evidence on AI's Labor Market Impact', 8 sector rows x (Sector | Technology Generation | Primary Finding | Anchor Studies), followed by a paragraph that explicitly reads the table as synthesis. The table's content survives the cleaner intact; only the 'Anchor Studies' citation column is emptied.

POLARIS is NOT the pure 'fact conveyor' the brief describes. Measured on the body: 363 sentences, 195 carry an [n] (53.7%), 168 carry none (46.3%). POLARIS ALREADY writes 46% unattributed/analysis sentences vs cellcog's 60%. The gap is 46%->60%, not 'fact conveyor vs mostly analysis'. The claim in SS3 that 'we stop at 7,742 words [because] a fact conveyor RUNS OUT OF THINGS TO SAY' is not supported by the sentence mix -- the deficit is in VISIBLE GROUNDING and STRUCTURE, not in willingness to write unsourced prose.
missed: ** THE ACTUAL CHEAPEST LEVER ON THE BOARD, AND NOBODY HAS NAMED IT: cellcog NARRATES ITS SOURCE-SELECTION CRITERIA IN PROSE, AND IT SURVIVES THE CLEANER 100%. ** cellcog SS1.2 'Scope, Methods, and Source Selection' reaches the judge verbatim: 'This review is restricted by design to peer-reviewed, English-language journal articles. The corpus was assembled through iterative database searches across economics journals (*Quarterly Journal of Economics*, *American Economic Review*, ...), management and operations journals (*Management Science*, ...), sociology and technology-studies journals (*Work, Employment and Society*, ...)... Approximately ninety-six articles comprise the core evidence base.' It names ~22 journals in ONE paragraph and asserts compliance in the instruction's own words. Task-72 has TWO criteria this paragraph directly answers -- 'Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles' (w=0.15) and 'Exclusive Citation of English-Language Journal Articles' (w=0.10) -- together 25% of the instruction_following dimension = 6.25% of overall. This is one paragraph of prose, and unlike the brief's transform it actually survives the cleaner. (Caveat that follows from my refutation #5: it is only honest for US once retrieval is journal-only.)

cellcog PRE-EMPTS the working-paper objection in prose, and that survives too: 'high-quality working papers -- notably Webb's patent-exposure measure -- are excluded even where they have shaped the field; where essential, we reference them as contextual rather than primary evidence.' It is performing instruction-compliance for the judge. POLARIS's opening paragraph instead narrates its own MACHINERY -- 'This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above... Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.' That paragraph talks about a pipeline, not about scholarship, and 'span-grounded to a cited source' is actively self-defeating once the cleaner has deleted every citation.

POLARIS places its markers AFTER the sentence-final period ('...routinized tasks.[15]') -- 218 of 240 occurrences. They are footnote-style appendages, syntactically OUTSIDE the clause: the maximally strippable position, and the format the cleaner is most explicitly told to remove. This is not a cosmetic detail -- it is the same structural property that killed probe variants A, B and D.

cellcog nests THREE heading levels: 9 H2 + 31 H3 + 8 H4 (the brief reports only the 31 H3). The 8 H4s sit under the two empirical-era sections (3.2.1-3.2.5, 3.3.1-3.3.5), i.e. depth is added exactly where evidence density is highest.

cellcog's abstract is a substantive 4-part structured abstract (**Objective** / **Methods** / **Findings** / **Contributions**) and it survives the cleaner in full. The brief's 'Executive summaries: NOT rewarded' should not be read as 'do not write one' -- the #1 system invests in a real one and it reaches the judge.

cellcog's bibliography is 104 entries (98 with DOI/URL), not 98 -- and it lists ~8 sources it never narratively cites. Its abstract self-reports 'approximately 96'. Minor, but it means 'cellcog has 98 and we have 97, so we are at parity on retrieval' is comparing a curated peer-reviewed corpus against our 97 (of which 66 are not journal articles). We are NOT at retrieval parity; we are at retrieval COUNT parity. That distinction is the whole ballgame for the 0.25-weight instruction-following dimension.

Reproduction artifacts, all on disk: /tmp/ver/cellcog_72_raw.md, /tmp/ver/cellcog_body.md, /tmp/ver/polaris_body.md, /tmp/ver/cellcog_CLEANED.md, /tmp/ver/polaris_CLEANED.md (real cleaner output), /tmp/ver/run_cleaner.py, /tmp/ver/probe.py (the 4-way A/B/C/D attribution-survival probe -- this is the cheap mechanism test the next round should re-run before committing to any compose).
